title: What Forgiveness Is. What It Isn't.
subtitle: A reading on forgiveness as a practical tool, not a moral obligation
excerpt: A reading on what forgiveness actually is and what it is not. Separates the psychological practice of forgiveness from the cultural and moral weight placed on it, and examines why the distinction matters for actually doing it.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on psychological research on forgiveness, including the work of Fred Luskin and Robert Enright.

practice.whenToUse: Use when forgiveness feels impossible, wrong, or demanded of you for reasons that make you resistant to it.

content.text: A reading on forgiveness. Forgiveness carries a large amount of cultural and moral loading that gets in the way of the practical question of whether it is something worth doing. This reading separates the concept from its freight and looks at what psychological research finds forgiveness to actually be.

Forgiveness is not condoning what happened. It is not saying the action was acceptable, that the harm doesn't matter, or that the person who caused it is now safe to trust or be around. It is not reconciliation: you can forgive someone without having any further contact with them. It is not a gift you give to the person who hurt you. It is not something owed to them.

It is not a process that happens once and ends. It is not something that must be declared out loud or shared with the person involved. It does not require them to have apologised, acknowledged the harm, or changed their behaviour.

Psychologists Fred Luskin and Robert Enright, who have studied forgiveness extensively, converge on a working definition: forgiveness is the decision to let go of resentment, bitterness, and the desire for punishment, for your own benefit. It is the release of the ongoing activation that holding the grievance produces in the nervous system.

The most clinically consistent finding in forgiveness research is that the person who benefits from forgiveness is the person who does the forgiving. Chronic resentment maintains a stress response: elevated cortisol, higher blood pressure, reduced immune function, lower mood. Forgiveness reduces that activation. Whether the other person ever knows is not the point.

The reason forgiveness is often refused, even when people understand the above, is that it can feel like a further injustice: the person who was wronged is now asked to do the work of releasing the wrongdoer. This is a real tension. The response to it is not to pretend the tension isn't there, but to be clear about what forgiveness is actually asking: not to excuse what happened, but to stop letting it run a stress response in your body for years after the event.
That is a different thing from absolution. It is a practical question about who is carrying what, and whether you want to continue carrying it.
Written for homemade.education. Draws on psychological research on forgiveness, including the work of Fred Luskin and Robert Enright.
